It is the code-sequenced **spine** and the sole **fan-out** point — it schedules nodes; owned-loop nodes never schedule child nodes.
whatItIsNot: Distinct from an owned-loop session, which owns work *inside* a node (ADR-0004, ADR-0005, ADR-0011).
seeAlso: _From `docs/glossary.md` and ADR-0001, ADR-0004, ADR-0005, ADR-0011; DBOS deferred by ADR-0019. See `spine` and `pi-adapter`._

title: spine
description: The code-sequenced control-flow layer (the orchestrator over plain Postgres, DBOS deferred — ADR-0019) that owns closed, deterministic routing: the order steps run in, when a loop iterates, which branch is taken.
glossaryBody:
The code-sequenced control-flow layer (the orchestrator over plain
Postgres — DBOS deferred, ADR-0019) that owns **closed, deterministic routing**:
the order steps run in, when a loop iterates, which branch is taken. The owned
loop is the **leaf** it delegates to. Discriminator (carried verbatim from
Agentic ADR-0026): *if a for-loop or a match could express the routing, the spine
owns it; if the routing needs the model to decide what comes next, the leaf
(owned-loop node) owns it.* Authoritatively defined by ADR-0005.
oneLine: The code-sequenced control-flow layer (the orchestrator over plain Postgres, DBOS deferred — ADR-0019) that owns closed, deterministic routing — the order steps run in, when a loop iterates, which branch is taken.
whatItIs: The code-sequenced control-flow layer (the orchestrator over plain Postgres — DBOS is deferred, ADR-0019) that owns **closed, deterministic routing**: the order steps run in, when a loop iterates, which branch is taken. The discriminator (carried verbatim from Agentic ADR-0026): *if a for-loop or a match could express the routing, the spine owns it; if the routing needs the model to decide what comes next, the leaf (owned-loop node) owns it.*
whatItIsNot: Not the **leaf** — the owned loop is the leaf the spine delegates to.
seeAlso: _From `docs/glossary.md`; authoritatively defined by ADR-0005 (carried verbatim from Agentic ADR-0026); DBOS deferred by ADR-0019. See `leaf-step-leaf-judgment` and `orchestrator`._

title: leaf step / leaf judgment
description: A single step in a code-sequenced cascade whose work is owned by the owned loop (what to write, how to satisfy a contract) rather than by the spine — the control-flow sense of "leaf"…
glossaryBody:
A single step in a code-sequenced cascade whose
work is owned by the **owned loop** (what to write, how to satisfy
a contract) rather than by the spine — the **control-flow** sense of "leaf"
(ADR-0005, ADR-0011). Distinct from **contract**, the *leaf tier* of the work hierarchy
(ADR-0002); the two senses must not be conflated.
oneLine: A single step in a code-sequenced cascade whose work is owned by the owned loop (what to write, how to satisfy a contract) rather than by the spine — the control-flow sense of "leaf".
whatItIs: A single step in a code-sequenced cascade whose work is owned by the **owned loop** (what to write, how to satisfy a contract) rather than by the spine — the **control-flow** sense of "leaf" (ADR-0005, ADR-0011).
whatItIsNot: Distinct from **contract**, the *leaf tier* of the work hierarchy (ADR-0002); the two senses of "leaf" must not be conflated.

title: agent package
description: The project-owned thin wrapper (`packages/agent`) and typed-event parser over the owned loop's surface (prompt / steer / follow-up + lifecycle events + edit-tool diffs).
glossaryBody:
The project-owned **thin wrapper** (`packages/agent`) and
typed-event parser over the owned loop's surface (prompt / steer / follow-up
+ lifecycle events + `edit`-tool diffs). The **sole** surface through which
the owned loop is invoked and the **only** place a model runtime is imported: it spawns/steers
the owned loop, normalizes its stream into the typed events the event store renders, and
exposes nothing model-shaped upward. No third-party agent framework sits in this
role; `packages/core` and `apps/studio` never parse the owned loop's stream directly
(ADR-0004, ADR-0006, ADR-0011). Carries v1's own-a-thin-wrapper-over-the-agent-runtime
principle (Agentic ADR-0008/0026).
oneLine: The project-owned thin wrapper (`packages/agent`) and typed-event parser over the owned loop's surface (`prompt` / `steer` / `follow-up` + lifecycle events + `edit`-tool diffs).
whatItIs: The project-owned **thin wrapper** (`packages/agent`) and typed-event parser over the owned loop's surface (`prompt` / `steer` / `follow-up` + lifecycle events + `edit`-tool diffs). It owns the loop on the Messages API and is the **sole** surface through which the owned loop is invoked and the **only** place a model runtime is imported: it spawns/steers the owned loop, normalizes its stream into the typed events the event store renders, and exposes nothing model-shaped upward.
whatItIsNot: No third-party agent framework sits in this role; `packages/core` and `apps/studio` never parse the owned loop's stream directly (ADR-0004, ADR-0006, ADR-0011).
seeAlso: _From `docs/glossary.md` and ADR-0004, ADR-0006, ADR-0011. Carries v1's own-a-thin-wrapper-over-the-agent-runtime principle (Agentic ADR-0008/0026). See `orchestrator` and `thin-wrapper-over-the-runtime`._

title: trunk
description: The canonical integrated mainline a capability lands on once approved.
glossaryBody:
The canonical **integrated mainline** a capability lands on once
**approved**. In v2 the trunk is **approval-gated** (a human admits a green
result), never auto-merge-on-green, and never holds knowingly-broken intermediate
states (ADR-0008). Supersedes v1's trunk, which auto-merged on green and tolerated
broken intermediate states under an eventual-consistency posture.
oneLine: The canonical integrated mainline a capability lands on once approved.
whatItIs: The canonical **integrated mainline** a capability lands on once **approved**. In v2 the trunk is **approval-gated** (a human admits a green result), never auto-merge-on-green, and never holds knowingly-broken intermediate states (ADR-0008).
whatItIsNot: Not v1's trunk, which it supersedes — v1's auto-merged on green and tolerated broken intermediate states under an eventual-consistency posture.

title: steering
description: A first-class, typed operator act of redirecting an in-flight owned-loop run mid-execution (the owned loop's steer operation), recorded as an event in the event store.
glossaryBody:
A first-class, typed operator act of **redirecting an in-flight
owned-loop run mid-execution** (the owned loop's steer operation), recorded as an event in the event
store. The in-loop counterpart to **approval**: the human shapes an action *while
it runs*, rather than only accepting/rejecting its result (ADR-0008).
oneLine: A first-class, typed operator act of redirecting an in-flight owned-loop run mid-execution (the owned loop's `steer` operation), recorded as an event in the event store.
whatItIs: A first-class, typed operator act of **redirecting an in-flight owned-loop run mid-execution** (the owned loop's `steer` operation), recorded as an event in the event store.
whatItIsNot: Distinct from **approval**: steering is the in-loop counterpart — the human shapes an action *while it runs*, rather than only accepting/rejecting its result (ADR-0008).

Newline-delimited JSON; the line-delimited record format (a candidate

In storytree, a **tree/game art asset** for the isometric renderer

title: Are the v1 "cautionary lessons" correctly housed as patterns?
description: auto-merge-on-green / vibe-the-load-bearing-layers / store-lock-races sit in an intentional lessons sub-bucket of `pattern` but describe mistakes, not approaches.
stakes: Our “mistakes to avoid” notes are shelved in the same drawer as our “recommended playbooks”, so a newcomer can’t tell a warning from an endorsed practice — and the Library risks reading its own past mistakes back as advice to repeat. We need to decide where the two remaining cautionary tales belong.
statement: Should the two remaining v1 cautionary lessons stay as `pattern`, move to `principle`, or merge into their positive counterparts?
context: `auto-merge-on-green` and `store-lock-races-and-id-collisions` sit under the seeder’s `// pattern: practices & cautionary lessons` framing but describe v1 mistakes, not reusable approaches you apply. Each overlaps a positive counterpart — the practice we adopted *instead*: `approval-gated-trunk`, and `claims-in-the-shared-store` / `durable-workflow-per-node`. (The third lesson, `vibe-the-load-bearing-layers`, was already merged into `own-the-layers` per ADR-0018 §D3 — the open-question-resolution ADR’s decision D3.)
diagram:
```
  cautionary lesson (a v1 mistake)         the practice we keep instead
  ────────────────────────────────         ────────────────────────────
  auto-merge-on-green                  ↔    approval-gated-trunk
  store-lock-races-and-id-collisions   ↔    claims-in-the-shared-store
                                            / durable-workflow-per-node
  vibe-the-load-bearing-layers         →    already folded into own-the-layers
                                            (ADR-0018 §D3)
```
Each lesson already has a positive twin; the question is whether the warning keeps its own shelf or is filed inside the twin.
options:
- **A — Keep + cross-link** each to its positive counterpart. Non-destructive; honours the intentional bucket.

- **B — Merge & retire**: fold each lesson’s v1 evidence into its counterpart’s "Why" and retire the standalone unit. DRY-est; loses standalone discoverability.

- **C — Recategorize → `principle`** (stated negatively). Keeps them standalone but stops calling them patterns.
recommendation: A (keep + cross-link). Non-binding until you decide.

title: Resolve, supersede, or retire draft ADR-0014?
description: ADR-0014 (notice-board feedback graduates into durable guidance) is still `draft`, yet the comments->events / studio<->store migration it anticipated is now being built — its status and surviving items need a call.
stakes: A decision is still stamped “draft” while the work it describes is already being built — like a policy stuck in “pending sign-off” after the team has moved on to a newer version of it. Until we file it, people keep citing an unsettled decision as if it were final.
statement: ADR-0014 (notice-board feedback -> durable guidance) is still `draft` with unresolved items, while its comments->events and studio<->store migration is now being built — should it be resolved (accepted/superseded), or retired, and which of its open items survive?
context: ADR-0014 framed how forum comments graduate into durable Library guidance. The migration it depends on is landing now: comments are moving into the event store (`events.comment`, with an `events.comment_event` table recording every change to a comment) and the studio is being wired to the shared Postgres store. A `draft` ADR the implementation has already overtaken is a drift risk — the build can diverge from a decision that was never settled, and downstream units cite it as if accepted. Its open items overlap newer ADRs (0017, which owns the knowledge/Library tier; 0018, the open-question → ADR resolution flow), so some may already be answered.
diagram:
```
  ADR-0014 (draft) ── describes ──► comments → events.comment migration
        │                            + studio ↔ Postgres wiring  (building NOW)
        │
        └─ open items overlap ──► ADR-0017  (owns the knowledge/Library tier)
                                  ADR-0018  (open-question → ADR flow)

  ⇒ lean: supersede 0014 BY 0017/0018, carry any still-live item forward
```
The newer ADRs already own what 0014 was reaching for; superseding keeps the lineage while the build stops citing a draft.
options:
- **A — Accept as-is**: ratify ADR-0014's current text and clear its open items. Cheapest paper-trail, but risks blessing claims the migration has already changed.

- **B — Supersede**: mark it superseded by ADR-0017/0018 (which own the knowledge tier and the open-question->ADR graduation), carrying forward only the still-live items as new open-questions. Cleanest lineage; most editing.

- **C — Retire**: withdraw ADR-0014 entirely as overtaken by events, and re-raise any genuinely-open item fresh. Least history to maintain, but loses the original rationale.
recommendation: Non-binding: lean B (supersede by ADR-0017/0018). The comments->events + studio<->store work is exactly what 0017/0018 govern, so 0014's durable-guidance intent is now carried there; supersede preserves lineage while the surviving items become first-class open-questions decided in the studio. The owner decides.
seeAlso: _ADR-0014 (doc:decisions/0014-notice-board-feedback-graduates-into-durable-guidance.md); supersession candidates ADR-0017 (knowledge tier) and ADR-0018 (open-question resolution). Related: `oq-library-doc-shape`._

title: How should the studio reconcile structured docs vs rendered edits?
description: The DB stores structured Knowledge docs, but the studio writes rendered GuidanceAssets, so editing a unit in the studio converts it to rendered form (one-way). Decide how structure survives editing.
stakes: Edit a Library entry in the studio today and it silently loses its structured fields — like an employee opening a fill-in-the-blanks form and the system saving it back as a freeform note with the field labels stripped out. The next rebuild from source then fights that edit, and one wins by accident.
statement: The store holds structured Knowledge docs (per-kind fields), but the studio writes rendered GuidanceAssets — so editing a unit in the studio collapses it to a rendered markdown body (one-way). How should the structured authoring source survive editing?
context: The studio’s save path (the `pg` write) stores each entry as a single rendered-markdown `body` and validates that string; the per-field structure (oneLine / whatItIs / options / …) is rebuilt on read by `renderBody()` — the core function that turns structured fields into the markdown you see — and CANNOT be recovered from the rendered body without parsing it back. `knowledge.json` stays the structured authoring source, but once an entry is edited in the studio its stored form is rendered-only, diverging from that source — which `build-corpus.mjs` (the generator that rebuilds the studio’s data from `knowledge.json`) would then overwrite or conflict with. ADR-0013 (“structured corpus, markdown as a view”) wants structure-as-source; ADR-0017 (the knowledge/Library tier) makes the templates derive from the schema. Editing in rendered form fights both.
diagram:
```
  knowledge.json ──build-corpus.mjs──► database row ──renderBody()──► fields shown
  (structured       (generator)          (rendered body,   (rebuilds the   in the studio
   source)                                structure dropped) field view)
       ▲                                       ▲
       │                                       │ studio EDIT saves a rendered body
       │  next rebuild CONFLICTS with ─────────┘ (one-way: the structured fields are gone)
       │  whatever the studio saved
```
`build-corpus.mjs` = the script that turns `knowledge.json` into what the studio reads; `renderBody()` = the function that rebuilds the field view on read.
options:
- **A — Accept rendered-on-edit + re-seed**: let studio edits store rendered bodies, and keep `knowledge.json` as the structured source that re-seeds structure on demand. Simplest to build (matches the current pg write); but edited units lose structured fields until re-seeded, and the two sources can drift.

- **B — Parse markdown back to structured on write**: the studio write reparses the rendered body into per-kind fields (inverse of `renderBody`) and stores a structured doc. Keeps one structured source; but the parser is fragile and must round-trip every kind exactly.

- **C — Edit structured fields directly**: the studio edits the per-kind fields (not a freeform body), so writes are always structured. Truest to ADR-0013/0017; largest client change (the wire shape the React client sees would have to carry fields, not just `body`).
recommendation: Non-binding: ship A now (it matches the additive, reversible pg write being built and keeps the client wire shape unchanged), and track B/C as the proper fix once the studio<->store loop is proven. A keeps `knowledge.json` authoritative for structure while unblocking shared live state. The owner decides.

title: Should STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE default to json or pg?
description: The studio backend is env-toggled between the offline `json` store and the live Cloud SQL `pg` store. Decide the default the toggle ships with.
stakes: When you start the studio, should it default to your own private copy of the data — works offline and costs nothing, but every session drifts apart — or the shared company database, where everyone sees the same thing but it must be switched on first and bills while running? `STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE` is that switch, and we need to pick its default.
statement: Should `STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE` default to `json` (offline-safe — the DB stays stopped, no cost, per-worktree state) or `pg` (shared live state across sessions — requires `pnpm db:up` and bills while up)?
context: The studio backend is being made switchable so the same `/api/*` contract serves either the offline `data/*.json` files or the live Postgres store. `STORYTREE_STUDIO_STORE` is that switch, and its default decides what a plain `pnpm dev` does. ADR-0015 keeps the Cloud SQL database STOPPED by default and bills only while it is up (you burst it on with `pnpm db:up`, work, then `pnpm db:down`); keyless IAM auth (ADR-0021) means there are no credentials to configure, but `pg` still needs the instance running. `json` is offline-safe but gives each worktree its own divergent copy — the very thing this migration aims to end.
diagram:
```
  pnpm dev (fresh start)
        │
        ├── default json ──► offline, $0, the DB stays off
        │                    BUT each session keeps its own copy → they drift apart
        │
        └── default pg ────► shared live state (the migration's goal)
                             BUT needs `pnpm db:up` first, and bills while up
```
The default trades “just works offline, but everyone diverges” against “shared by default, but costs and needs the DB up.”
options:
- **A — Default `json`**: offline-safe, zero cost, no DB prerequisite; a fresh clone just works. But shared live state (the migration's goal) is opt-in, so most sessions keep diverging.

- **B — Default `pg`**: sessions share live state by default — the dogfooding win. But `pnpm dev` now fails unless the instance is up (cost + a `db:up` step), breaking offline work.
recommendation: Non-binding: default `json` while the pg path is new and human-verified-after-the-fact — it preserves offline dev and the stop-by-default cost posture, and `pg` stays a deliberate opt-in for sessions that want shared state. Revisit once the studio<->store loop is proven and `db:up` is routine. The owner decides.
seeAlso: _ADR-0015 (doc:decisions/0015-gcp-hosting-cloud-sql-event-store.md) stop/start cost posture; ADR-0021 (keyless IAM). Related: `oq-library-doc-shape`, `stack-cloud-sql-keyless-iam`._

title: Reconcile the YAML-as-source claim with the JSON/markdown reality?
description: ADR-0013 says YAML is the source of truth, but the Library's structured source on disk is JSON (knowledge.json) and stories/ are frontmatter-.md. Reconcile the formats or document the split as intended.
stakes: Our filing policy (ADR-0013) says every record is kept in YAML, but the Library actually files in JSON and the stories in markdown — an employee handbook naming a filing system nobody uses. Anyone who trusts the handbook hunts for YAML that isn’t there, and any tool built on that assumption breaks.
statement: ADR-0013 says 'YAML is the source of truth', but the Library's structured source on disk is JSON (`apps/studio/data/knowledge.json`) and the `stories/` corpus is frontmatter-`.md` — should the formats be reconciled (commit to one) or the split documented as intended?
context: ADR-0013 (“structured corpus, markdown as a view”) asserts YAML as the authoring source of truth. In practice the Library authors in JSON (`knowledge.json`, consumed by the `build-corpus.mjs` generator) and the story tier authors in frontmatter-markdown (`stories/`). No YAML is the source of either. The mismatch is a documentation/decision drift: a reader of ADR-0013 expects YAML and finds none, and tooling that assumed YAML would be wrong. Either the ADR is stale, or the per-tier format split is deliberate and just undocumented.
diagram:
```
  ADR-0013 says:  "YAML is the source of truth"  ──on disk──►  (no YAML anywhere)

  Library tier  ──source──►  knowledge.json          ──build-corpus.mjs──►  rendered
                             (JSON)                                          markdown view
  Story tier    ──source──►  stories/*.md             ─────────────────────►  rendered
                             (frontmatter-markdown)                            as-is
```
Reality is a per-tier split (JSON + markdown), not YAML — the cheap fix is to make the ADR say so.
options:
- **A — Commit to one format**: pick a single structured source format (e.g. JSON everywhere, or YAML everywhere) and migrate, then ADR-0013 is literally true. Cleanest mental model; real migration cost and churn in build tooling.

- **B — Document the split as intended**: amend ADR-0013 to state that the structured source is JSON for the Library and frontmatter-`.md` for stories (markdown still the rendered view), and that 'YAML' was illustrative. No migration; the ADR matches reality. Accepts heterogeneity.

- **C — Defer**: leave it, accept the drift for now. Cheapest, but the stale ADR keeps misleading readers.
recommendation: Non-binding: B (document the split). The JSON/markdown split already works and feeds the generators; the cheap, honest fix is to update ADR-0013 to describe the per-tier formats rather than force a migration whose only benefit is making one sentence literally true. The owner decides.
seeAlso: _ADR-0013 (doc:decisions/0013-structured-corpus-markdown-as-view.md) the YAML-as-source claim; ADR-0017 (knowledge tier, structured source). Related: `oq-library-doc-shape`._

title: Cloud SQL access · Node connector · keyless IAM
description: How storytree reaches its Postgres runtime store: the Cloud SQL Node connector with IAM database auth via ambient ADC. Agent sessions need no per-session OAuth and no key files.
statement: Reach the Cloud SQL Postgres via the Node connector with keyless IAM database authentication — agent sessions authenticate from ambient Application Default Credentials, never a per-session OAuth or a key file.
whatItIs: The `@google-cloud/cloud-sql-connector` Node connector (paired with `pg`, in `packages/store`) mints short-lived IAM tokens as the database password over a TLS socket — no password is stored. Identity comes from the operator’s Application Default Credentials already established on the dev host (plus a dedicated service account for headless/impersonated runs). The IAM database user is the operator email (`STORYTREE_DB_USER`), the database is `storytree`, and the instance connection name is `storytree-498613:australia-southeast1:storytree-pg`.
whyThis: No secrets in the repo and no per-session interactive login, so agents can run migrations and queries headlessly; it matches the stop/start cost posture (ADR-0015 §5) and keeps the browser/credential-free boundary (the studio never holds DB creds). A session should ASSUME keyless credentials are present and verify with `gcloud auth application-default print-access-token`, not assume it is unauthenticated.
constraints: The instance is STOPPED by default and bills only while up — burst with `pnpm db:up` → work → `pnpm db:down`. Auto-stop is **idle-aware** (ADR-0015 §5, `infra/idle-stop.tf`): a Cloud Function stops the instance only after ~60 min with zero DB connections, so it never stops a live session (a connection held by a session / the Auth Proxy resets the idle timer); a once-a-day blunt cron is the cost floor behind it. So a long idle gap will stop the DB — re-`db:up` if a query fails to connect. IAM users are bare roles, so a ONE-TIME privilege bootstrap (`infra/bootstrap-grants.sql`, applied as the throwaway-password `postgres` user via `packages/store/scripts/apply-grants.mjs`) granted the IAM user `CREATE` on the database; this is idempotent and already applied. The connector deps live in `packages/store`; DBOS is deferred (ADR-0019), so this is a plain typed Postgres connection.
seeAlso: _Synthesised from ADR-0021 (doc:decisions/0021-keyless-agent-session-auth-and-db-bootstrap.md) and ADR-0015 (doc:decisions/0015-gcp-hosting-cloud-sql-event-store.md). Validated 2026-06-08: 73 units migrated keyless, then the instance stopped._
